comments.author: Daria Pardue
comments.body: Hi [~nick@nickrobillard.com], thank you for reporting this! After investigation, it appears that the correct behavior, consistent with our shared drivers spec, should actually be `null` and not `undefined` when no results are found, so we'll be putting a fix into the next patch release of the driver. We apologize for any inconvenience or confusion this may have caused!

comments.author: Eric Adum

comments.author: Daria Pardue
comments.body: [~jerren_s@hotmail.com] Hi, just to give you an update, we determined that the `fullResponse` option was actually exposed in the driver by mistake - its user-facing functionality is a feature that was never implemented and that we currently have no plans to implement, hence we'll be deprecating it in our next release and removing any associated codepaths (i.e., it will do nothing at all when passed to an operation, instead of causing unexpected behavior).

comments.author: Daria Pardue
comments.body: [~haser@videmo.de] While investigating a related issue, we discovered that we inadvertently enable command monitoring by default in v4 of the driver (see NODE-3513), while this may not account for 100% of the performance degradation reported here, it should account for a significant portion of it. We'll be releasing a fix next week, in the meanwhile, explicitly disabling command monitoring (by passing in { {{monitorCommands: false}} } in the MongoClient constructor options) should improve the performance by a considerable margin.

comments.author: Yannick Haser

comments.author: Daria Pardue
comments.body: [~haser@videmo.de] Another update: we recently tracked down the root cause of the remaining performance issues to the bson library serialization/deserialization methods: the node v4 driver uses js-bson v4 (bson@4.4.1), whereas the node v3 driver uses js-bson v1 (bson@1.1.6). Unfortunately, bson@1.1.6 is not compatible with driver v4, so downgrading the bson library would not be a viable workaround for the issue. We are currently investigating performance improvements to the bson library, which should resolve the observed performance issues with the node driver.

comments.author: Daria Pardue
comments.body: The issue has been fixed in bson@4.5.0. Users of existing 4.x versions of the node driver can pull in the fix by doing a clean npm install, which will pull in the 4.5.0 bson dependency. Furthermore, the next version of the node driver (4.1.1) will update the minimum required bson version to 4.5.0.

comments.author: Kaitlin Mahar
comments.body:
Hi [~gabor42mezo@gmail.com], thank you for reaching out.

This issue actually results from a longstanding bug in the MongoDB server (see SERVER-24007) where error messages in this particular case can contain invalid UTF-8. 

The check you are referring to commenting out is validating that the string being parsed is valid UTF-8. In general, the BSON format requires that all strings are valid UTF-8 which is why this is in place.

This is newly showing up in driver 4.1 for you as driver 3.5 relied on js-bson 1.x and driver 4.1 relies on js-bson 4.x, and the change was introduced in js-bson 2.0.

While we wait for SERVER-24007 to be fixed, the solution for us here which some other MongoDB drivers have implemented is for to skip the validation in the particular case where we are decoding a string returned as a server error message.

Doing this will require some refactoring on our part and a bit more investigation is needed to determine the best way to implement that. We will updat
[...truncated 92 chars]

comments.author: Martin Bajana
comments.body: Hi [~chiodi84@gmail.com], thanks for reaching out. When specifying your own _id type in the collection model, you should not make it optional, because if you insert an object without specifying the _id, MongoDB will create an ObjectId type _id on your behalf, leading to an inconsistent schema in your database. The problem you are seeing is due to the way the types for the _id are currently reconciled (see NODE-3761).  Note that it is appropriate for the insert to require an _id because you have a custom _id.

comments.author: Paolo Chiodi
comments.body:
Hi @Martin Bajana, thanks for the response.

I agree the _id should not be optional, but I do not understand why insert should require it. 
The nodejs driver has the pkFactory.createPk option that controls how the _id is generated. 
When using that option, shouldn't we be able to infer records without an explicit _id?

comments.author: Martin Bajana
comments.body: [~chiodi84@gmail.com] Thanks for bringing this to our attention.  We don't currently account for the createPk in the typings and we will take this back to explore the available options to address it in an upcoming release.

comments.author: Daria Pardue
comments.body:
Docs changes needed to the typescript guidance page to clarify that when specifying a collection schema:
* _id not defined on collection means the default ObjectId will be used, and won't be required on inserts;
* _id defined as required on a collection means that the user will provide the _id on every insert
* _id defined as optional means that the user is expecting to rely on a pkFactory method to generate a consistent id, so it will not be required to be specified explicitly on inserts

comments.author: Durran Jordan
comments.body:
Just to update status for the ticket [~sergeyp@brightdata.com] .

 

I have found the core issue here from the change we introduced in [https://github.com/mongodb/node-mongodb-native/pull/3256] . Once we started potentially skipping {{hello}} responses in the monitoring connections to take the last one as the source of truth, when streaming protocol messages do get skipped the {{responseId}} of the last hello does not match the {{requestId}} of the initial hello and we cannot match on the {{operationDescription}} in the connection's internal queue. This causes all subsequent hello responses from that specific monitoring connection to be skipped. (https://jira.mongodb.org/browse/SERVER-45775 explains this behaviour but won't change on the server side)

 

In this specific case, when the step down occurs, the former primary sends 3 immediate {{hello}} responses in succession, and the last one is parsed but it falls into this scenario. It subsequently stays reported as a secondary
[...truncated 387 chars]

[~redixhumayun@gmail.com], just to clarify, ticket assignment would be something the team would handle internally to ensure visibility of scheduled work by the MongoDB Node.js driver engineers.

If you're interested in working on a bug we'd recommend:

1. Reviewing the [project's Repository|https://github.com/mongodb/node-mongodb-native]

comments.author: Yuval Aloni
comments.body:
'iterating other' should be: 'iterating over'.
Calling `getInsertedIds()` also returns the non-inserted document id.

comments.author: Durran Jordan
comments.body:
I can confirm that {{insertedIds}} is the list of ids that were added to the {{BulkWriteResult}} at the point of when the command is sent. Since we are generating that list on the client side we don't go back and edit that list based on the error. Our CRUD specification does not specify any behaviour around this and that field is actually optional for the driver.

It seems for ordered bulk writes, we could potentially prune the list of ids based on the count of inserted documents. For unordered that approach would not be possible so we'd need to discuss how to approach and if it's viable. Sending this to triage.

comments.author: Yuval Aloni
comments.body:
BTW; according to the docs
any item of (the native) BulkWriteResult.writeErrors has an index property in the bulk operations list — 
https://www.mongodb.com/docs/manual/reference/method/BulkWriteResult/#mongodb-data-BulkWriteResult.writeErrors.index

so it should be possible to retrieve the relevant _id from that list using that, and remove it from insertedIds, in both ordered and unordered.

As someone who makes use of the driver I currently 
do a
.catch
and "manually" remove ids from insertedIds if they are present in writeErrors, then return the modified result, then do a .then which always gets a "correct" result object.

comments.author: Alex Bevilacqua
comments.body:
Hi [~yuval.invoke@gmail.com], thank you for raising this ticket. As [~durran.jordan@mongodb.com] indicated the [CRUD specification|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/9f6848712ee5b66bca8562c8903ecd6a546bcd63/source/crud/crud.rst#id25] indicates that {{insertedIds}} are optional, however as it is possible (though not ideal) to cross reference these with [WriteErrors|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/9f6848712ee5b66bca8562c8903ecd6a546bcd63/source/crud/crud.rst#writeerror] there may be an opportunity to improve this experience.

I'm going to review this further and will circle back with additional details.

comments.author: Yuval Aloni
comments.body:
[~alex.bevilacqua@mongodb.com] thanks. Appreciate it.
A further note after looking at the CRUD specs you linked - 
it states (in the comment above it, explaining it) that each value is the id of ??the inserted document??

which, I believe, further emphasis that those, if included, should reflect actual inserted documents, and exclude ids of documents that weren't (because of write errors or any reason).

Hey [~RollingSnack831@gmail.com] - this is a duplicate of NODE-4664.  Basically, the UpdateFilter operator breaks when a schema with `[key: string]: any` is provided.

Extending `Document` as in your example isn't a use case we've considered.  Would you mind providing some context about why this is useful for your application?

comments.author: Snack N/A
comments.body:
Thanks [~bailey.pearson@mongodb.com] for the replay. I noticed that ISSUE(4664) before, but not quite sure if they have the same cause.

A definition of `{{{}interface{}}}` is too free, so our team uses `{{{}extends Document{}}}` on an `{{{}interface{}}}` to describe a database document, conventionally.

Our members were all new to this area, so we did not foresee these issues. Since it is not a use case under the consideration and our project is still in its infancy, we might be able to just switch to another solution.

comments.author: Garr Godfrey
comments.body:
I've positively identified where the hang is. It is in `readMany` waiting for the first message to be read. It does seem to impact many connections at once and does not involve disconnections or socket errors of any kind. 
The connections are still alive on the server side, when the issue can first be detected, but they are inactive, having fully sent their responses long ago.
I've digging down to the SizedMessageTransform to investigate how many bytes were actually received

comments.author: Garr Godfrey
comments.body:
[~neal.beeken@mongodb.com]

I found the problem and have a probable solution.

I tracked down into SizedMessageTransform and the onData emitter. I put in code to count the number of messages sent through SizedMessageTransform and those received by the emitter. For each connection that gets into a hung state, they are off by one, with one message being lost (sent from SizedMessageTransform, but never read in the onData handler).

The explanation for this is that we have an `await` between when the socket is written to, and when the onData class adds the listener to the 'data' event. The data event gets sent and there is nobody listening for it!!

I confirmed this by tracking whether the connection had passed the "drained" yet when a message is returned by SizedMessageTransform. Every time it would get a message but the connection had not yet received the "drained" event, that connection would then hang.

I further verified by removing the await for the 'drain' in the writeComm
[...truncated 708 chars]

comments.author: Neal Beeken
comments.body:
Hey [~sergeyp@brightdata.com], 

Surrounding the "read documents: 9284" lines are topology description events that show "ReplicaSetNoPrimary" which means the driver cannot run the operations. The host we expect to become the primary again is {{27020}} so if you look for "{{me: '127.0.0.1:27020'}}" which are heartbeat success events you will see that that host only reports itself as the primary after 29 seconds of when we printed  "2025-02-13T07:55:29.850Z expecting step up ===". The driver is reacting pretty much as soon as it is told of the election, but it seems that after the 60 second step down expires it takes some time for the election to occur and for the driver to be made aware. 

Maybe you can coordinate the timestamps from here with the server logs to identify if the server is actually taking 20sec to re-elect after stepDown expires. Since, our "expecting step up" line is soley based on 60sec having gone by, the server needs to perform operations to make the election happ
[...truncated 139 chars]

comments.author: TPM Jira Automations Bot

comments.author: Aditi Khare
comments.body:
Hi [~kduffie@navu.co],

Thank you for bringing this issue to our attention!

The {{ignoreUndefined}} option is working as designed. In your specific case, since {{{}ignoreUndefined=true{}}}, the driver does not detect that all atomic operators are undefined. This conflicts with [a common MongoDB drivers requirement|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/ce35696db1c8da0e15a067b8022c853f1d6d1292/source/crud/bulk-write.md#update-vs-replace-document-validation], which makes the server to perform a {{{}replace{}}}, rather than the operation failing altogether.

In the future, the team plans to address the behavior of {{ignoreUndefined}} for this edge case. For now, it is recommended to always include at least one defined atomic operator when attempting an {{update}} operation.

comments.author: Kingston Duffie
comments.body:
Thanks for reviewing.  My workaround is, as you suggest, that I avoid calling updateOne in these cases where there are no items in any clause.  I believe that this warrants attention because of one simple fact.  It is easy to accidentally lose all of the contents of a record.  If there are no terms in an $addToSet clause, did I really want to effectively delete the entire document??  Of course not.

Saying that this "works as designed" doesn't really cut it.  But I completely understand and will appreciate when you do revisit the ignoreUndefined implementation.

comments.author: Aditi Khare
comments.body:
Hi again [~kduffie@navu.co],

To clarify, to avoid accidental operations, the preferred workaround would be to keep the default setting {{{}ignoreUndefined=false{}}}. 

By setting {{{}ignoreUndefined=true{}}}, you're overriding the check performed by the update API. We're intending to address this in the long term by removing the ability to override the atomic operator check.

comments.author: Kingston Duffie
comments.body:
It looks like you're waiting for a reply from me.   Suffice it to say that I acknowledge your response – although I disagree with your conclusion.   

Ask yourself what I mean when I do this: 

collection.updateOne(\{_id: recordId}, \{$set: {}});

Well, it seems like a null operation to me.  I'd expect either an error or that the document remained unchanged.

Now ask what I mean when I do this:

collection.updateOne(\{_id: recordId), {$set: undefined});

Again, there are only two rational interpretations – that I've made an error or that I want the document unchanged.

So now replace $set with $addToSet or $push and it is exactly the same.

So why ever support undefined?  Just think about how I write the code that updates a document in a variety of different ways in a single update conditional on a variety of terms.  Consider an update like this:

collection.updateOne(\{_id: recordId}, \{$set: {a: 1, b: 2}, $push: \{c: "x"}, $addToSet: \{d: "y"}})

In reality, this 
[...truncated 478 chars]

comments.author: Daria Pardue
comments.body: [~kduffie@navu.co] Thanks for sharing your use case for `ignoreUndefined`! We will take your use case into consideration as we decide how to best approach balancing this Node driver specific option's convenience with the potential unintended consequences, particularly when used in conjunction with driver features whose behavior is defined by the common drivers specification. Rest assured that regardless of the long term solution, we'll be looking to fix the specific issue that you reported in a non-breaking way for the current driver version. You can continue to follow this ticket for the scheduling and status of the corresponding fix.

comments.author: Kingston Duffie
comments.body: I certainly can't ask for more than that.  And I appreciate your attention to this.

This issue was introduced in [this commit|#diff-0856a8276a8d7aa66f2258b89c22e6f7c8a8c7e5b1b7c5c5b7af25dfbe864cc0R70],] before we had property commonWireVersion defined as nullable (commonWireVersion?: number), and after the change it becomes number (commonWireVersion: number), 0 by default. comments.author: Peter Vandercappellen
comments.body:
I noticed this too and made the following observations when comparing the 2.1.21 vs 2.1.18 driver behavior:

- Old driver only fires a reconnect event when the connection to the server can be established again. The new driver does not, but while connected seems to frequently fire this event.
- New driver (with any luck) reports a network error in eg: the "toArray" method of the Cursor. All calls after this do not return/callback anything (black-hole?)
- Old driver behavior is similar to the previous observation. First time error, after that the queries seem to get buffered until the connection can be opened again. It does not time-out. Is this the intended behavior? If so, could this be changed in a future release? In my case it was tied to a website and the requests kept piling up while the Mongo-db was unavailable for an hour!

comments.author: Christian Amor Kvalheim
comments.body: you can try the next pre-release 2.2.0-alpha1 that got pushed to npm today

comments.author: Christian Amor Kvalheim
comments.body:
The buffering behavior will not change as it's critical for failovers and brief service interruptions to avoid a cascade of errors and has been the expected behavior for years. You can disable it if you want to receive all errors by setting the option 

bufferMaxEntries to 0

comments.author: Christian Amor Kvalheim
comments.body: you are aware that secondary reads in sharded cluster can cause duplicates if you have the balancer running.

comments.author: Boyan Kirov
comments.body:
Yes, we are aware of the consequences when reading from a secondary (which can include duplicates, stale data, almost "scratching" the High Availability idea and etc.). We're doing evaluation and we want to collect all pros and cons so that we can make a well-informed decision on how to move forward.

Nevertheless it seems that the Node.js driver should correctly pass the supported option as the Python one does.

comments.author: Christian Amor Kvalheim
comments.body:
This is legacy behavior the recommended way is to use the chainable methods on the cursor. You are highly encourage to not use the options as the code around this is brittle and only there to ensure no backward breaking changes.

/**
 * Creates a new Cursor instance (INTERNAL TYPE, do not instantiate directly)
 * @class Cursor
 * @extends external:CoreCursor
 * @extends external:Readable
 * @property {string} sortValue Cursor query sort setting.
 * @property {boolean} timeout Is Cursor able to time out.
 * @property {ReadPreference} readPreference Get cursor ReadPreference.
 * @fires Cursor#data
 * @fires Cursor#end
 * @fires Cursor#close
 * @fires Cursor#readable
 * @return {Cursor} a Cursor instance.
 * @example
 * Cursor cursor options.
 *
 * collection.find({}).project({a:1})                             // Create a projection of field a
 * collection.find({}).skip(1).limit(10)                          // Skip 1 and limit 10
 * collection.find({}).batchSize(5)   
[...truncated 1950 chars]

comments.author: Christian Amor Kvalheim
Jon Helge Stensrud
comments.body:
Thank you for your reponse!

I agree that chaining generally is a better approach. We started getting error messages when testing MongoDB 3.2, and it took some time tracking down the reason (I don't study the mongodb driver regularly...). These errors seem to fail silently in MongoDB 3.0, making me think more teams than ours are going to experience these problems when moving on to 3.2. I would hope for a deprecation warning, or a better detection algorithm. Please let me know if you want me to submit a pull request.

comments.author: Christian Amor Kvalheim

comments.author: Jon Helge Stensrud
comments.body:
Ok. I will give it a shot.

Do you have any input on the algorithm? It cannot possibly work in all edge-cases.

I'm thinking, assume options if:
- none of the keys are known options (as today)
or
- all of the values are 0 or 1, and not all keys are known keys

There could be logic in there on valid values on all the options, like "fields: 1" is invalid and thus probably not meant as an option, but that might be over-complicating things and harder to maintain...

comments.body: Cross-referencing a PR discussion about pipeline validation (and differentiating DBRef objects) from PHPLIB-418.

comments.author: Jeremy Mikola
comments.body:
As noted in DRIVERS-2638: libmongoc's [{{_mongoc_document_is_pipeline}}|https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-c-driver/blob/1.23.5/src/libmongoc/src/mongoc/mongoc-util.c#L655] utility function does _not_ consider an empty array to be an update pipeline. This determines how {{update}} parameters are appended in outgoing command documents for {{update}} and {{findAndModify}} commands. See:

 * [{{_mongoc_write_command_update_append}}|https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-c-driver/blob/1.23.5/src/libmongoc/src/mongoc/mongoc-write-command.c#L123]
 * [{{mongoc_collection_find_and_modify_with_opts}}|https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-c-driver/blob/1.23.5/src/libmongoc/src/mongoc/mongoc-collection.c#L3449]

This seems like an intentional decision done for BC reasons. Since BSON documents and arrays are both represented using {{bson_t}} structs, empty {{bson_t}} structs would historically have been interpreted as empty replacement documents instead of NOP pipelines.

----

PHPLIB has the ability

Message: PHPLIB-1129: Improve pipeline checks for replace operations (#1098)

* Improve pipeline checks for replace operations

Consolidates data providers in base TestCase and FunctionalTestCase classes.

Change is_pipeline() to conditionally consider empty arrays as pipelines. This was needed for improvements to replace/update parameter validation, and will prove useful for consolidated pipeline validation in PHPLIB-881.

Prohibit update documents and pipelines (both empty and non-empty) for replacement operations.

Prohibit replacement documents and empty pipeines for update operations. Empty pipelines must be prohibited because libmongoc does not support them for update commands (they're indistinguishable from empty replacement documents). For consistency, we also prohibit them for FindOneAndUpdate even though PHPLIB could work around that.

comments.author: Derick Rethans
comments.body: This should be documented better at http://mongodb.github.io/mongo-php-library/api/class-MongoDB.Collection.html#_find, but the second argument to the ``find()`` method accepts $options. These options can include sort and limit, as is demonstrated at http://php.net/manual/en/mongodb-driver-query.construct.php under "queryOptions". There is an example of how the options look like at refsect1-mongodb-driver-query.construct-examples. These same options you can use for the library find() method.

comments.author: Jeremy Mikola
comments.body: The current documentation for [{{MongoDB\Collection::find()}}|https://docs.mongodb.com/php-library/master/reference/method/MongoDBCollection-find/] documents the {{limit}} option and also has an example of applying a limit to a query. I think this can be closed.

comments.author: Jeremy Mikola
comments.body:
One idea:

 * Implement {{Cursor::getSession()}}, which can be used to access either the explicit or implicit session associated with a cursor
 * Have Session track whether or not it is implicit
 * Implement {{Session::isImplicit()}} so that PHPLIB can access the nature of the session

With this approach, ChangeStream operation would not create an "implicit from the user's perspective" session on its own, but rather rely on PHPC to do so internally. It would then capture the Cursor's Session immediately after executing the aggregate command and stash that for use by the resume callback. This may require reproducing [libmongoc's logic|https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-c-driver/commit/5415c883c3ea1e99a09644ddbd4f201b369f40a5#diff-77c20ef1f755ee1119639cf4ad8e33ddR371] in the ChangeStream iterator.

comments.author: Jeremy Mikola
comments.body:
After talking this over with [~kevin.albertson], I think there is a viable approach which allows this to be handled entirely in PHPLIB.

 * ChangeStream operation never relies on PHPC or libmongoc to create an implicit session. If the user doesn't specify an explicit "session" option, it should create its own session to be as an "implicit from the user's perspective" session. This means that PHPLIB effectively mimics the logic we're employing in PHPC-1152 to ensure session pinning for regular aggregate commands.
 * This session is used for the original aggregate command and future resumes. Ensuring that aggregate and ensuring getMore commands on the same cursor share the same session will be satisifed by PHPC-1152.
 * The ChangeStream operation and iterator will now be responsible for freeing the "implicit from the user's perspective" session if {{next()}} sees that the cursor ID is zero ([as is done in libmongoc|https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-c-driver/commit/5415c883c3ea1e99a096
[...truncated 537 chars]

comments.author: Jeremy Mikola
comments.body:
New revelation: PHPLIB shouldn't actually need to worry about freeing the Session within the Cursor. Provided the ChangeStream operation unsets its own reference to the "implicit" session it creates once its {{next()}} method observes that the cursor's ID is zero, PHPC-1152 can perform the same check in {{php_phongo_cursor_iterator_move_forward()}} and remove a reference to the Session object once _it_ observes that the cursor's ID is zero. Combined, this will ensure that the Session is freed as soon as possible, in compliance with the [Server Session spec|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/master/source/sessions/driver-sessions.rst#server-session-pool]:

{quote}
An implicit session MUST be returned to the pool immediately following the completion of an operation. When an implicit session is associated with a cursor for use with getMore operations, the session MUST be returned to the pool immediately following a getMore operation that indicates that the cursor has been e
[...truncated 759 chars]

comments.author: Andreas Braun
comments.body: [~jmikola]: There are two different issues here. One is that the default transaction options should inherit the original read concern, read preference, and write concern from the Client object. This is simple enough. The second issue is that when running an operation with a session, the transaction options there must override any options from the client, database, or collection objects. This can't easily be implemented, as neither PHPC nor libmongoc expose the transaction options. I suggest we fix the first part as that has no upstream dependencies and discuss necessary upstream changes for the second part without fixing them in 1.5.0.

comments.author: Jeremy Mikola
comments.body:
[~andreas.braun]: That sounds reasonable. Please create a second PHPLIB ticket for ensuring transaction options can override client, database, and collection and feel free to revise the title/description of this one.

I think two additional tickets will also be needed: a PHPC ticket for the Session to expose its options (or at least default transaction options) and a second CDRIVER ticket for the necessary upstream API. Hopefully that CDRIVER issue can be scheduled for 1.16.

comments.author: Andreas Braun
comments.body:
After discussion, we can work on this already and work around the limitations currently imposed by missing accessors.

The reason we always add the {{readConcern}}, {{readPreference}}, and {{writeConcern}} options when running an operation is that libmongoc does not know about our {{Database}} and {{Collection}} objects' options. However, when in a transaction we don't need to use these, as libmongoc will automatically use the correct options from the currently running transaction. The solution for those options is to simply not inherit them if we're in a transaction.

The downside of this is that server selection in PHPLIB currently relies on the read preference being given, which we'd need to extract from the session. However, as of MongoDB 4.2 the spec still specifies that only a read preference of {{primary}} is allowed within a transaction, so we can get away with defaulting to primary for server selection within a transaction. This works around the missing accessors until the
[...truncated 94 chars]

comments.author: Jeremy Mikola
comments.body:
Some additional investigation on this indicates points back to PHPC-1050. Previously, simply constructing a command cursor might trigger a {{getMore}} if the {{firstBatch}} was empty. With that change, we deferred the advance-and-check-for-error to {{rewind()}} or the first call to {{next()}}.

I don't believe PHPC-1050 was incorrect, but a better fix may be to see if we can avoid ever triggering a {{getMore}} when rewinding a cursor. Given that the purpose of rewind is simply to position ourselves at the first result, it doesn't make sense to have to communicate the the server. If {{firstBatch}} is empty, there is neither any result to advance to nor is there any error to check. If {{firstBatch}} is non-empty, then advancing should be entirely a client-side operation.

Note: this may prove difficult, as libmongoc only provides an API for advancing with {{mongoc_cursor_next()}}.

comments.author: Jeremy Mikola
comments.body: Bumping back to 1.5.0. While this is a bug fix, it's also a subtle behavioral change and would be best introduced in a new minor version.

comments.body:
Author:{'name': 'Jeremy Mikola', 'username': 'jmikola', 'email': 'jmikola@gmail.com'}
Message: PHPLIB-451: ChangeStream::rewind() should never execute getMore

Tests were revised to no longer expect rewind() to encounter an error and resume. In most tests, rewind() is a NOP because the change stream has no events to return upon creation.

Since Watch now always executes aggregate commands with APM, the check for startAtOperationTime support was moved to createResumeCallable(). We continue to only capture operationTime from the very first aggregate command.

Various assertions for current() returning null were also changed to instead check valid(). Both forms are equivalent, but checking valid() is more consistent with our iteration examples.
Branch: master

comments.author: Durran Jordan
comments.body: [~agis] Would you like to try again against master? I've done a good amount of tweaking now.

comments.author: Agis
comments.body:
Hi Durran, thanks for looking into this.

I've tested against master; results are at https://gist.github.com/agis-/263a76a3fc443a8973de#file-results-master-md.

While 2.1.0 w/ monitoring is better compared to before, 2.1.0 in general is still way behind 1.8. I've also added some cpu profiles: https://gist.github.com/agis-/263a76a3fc443a8973de#file-stackprof-md.

It seems that a fair amount of time is spent deserializing BSON in 2.1.0 (with bson 3.2), while this was not the case in 1.8 (with bson 1.8 & bson_ext).

Thanks!

comments.author: Durran Jordan
comments.body: Yes the deserialization code in the new BSON was slower in favor of a more extensible design. There are still improvements that can be done in BSON - for example we will be replacing the string bytes manipulation with a proper native byte buffer, but it's going to take a little bit of time to do that... It won't be a fix that goes in this week. I'll continue to try and tweak the native code there without an API change before the byte buffer implementation goes in.

comments.author: Antoine Joulie
comments.body:
This line seem odd to me : https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-ruby-driver/blob/v2.1.2/lib/mongo/cluster/topology/replica_set.rb#L145

If I remove the `!has_primary?` condition everything work. Of course, I am sure there is some good reason for this condition to be there. 
Just saying it sometime happens that there is a primary server and replica set topology changes, the change won't be take in account. 

Feel free to ask for more explanation, if needed.

Thanks

comments.author: Emily Stolfo
comments.body:
Hi [~ajoulie]

Firstly, [this spec|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/master/source/server-discovery-and-monitoring/server-discovery-and-monitoring.rst] may be helpful for providing a detailed explanation of how the driver updates its view of a cluster if there are changes in its state.

Regarding the !has_primary? check: this ensures that the driver does not take into account stale reports of topology state from servers. If a particular server reports that there is an additional host in the replica set but the current driver topology has a primary, the addition will not be taken into account. [This particular test|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/master/source/server-discovery-and-monitoring/tests/rs/discovery.yml] shows the exact scenario in which you must check if the current topology has a primary before taking into account additional hosts reported by a server. If I take out the 'has_primary?' check in Topology::ReplicaSet, [this part|https://github.c
[...truncated 464 chars]

comments.author: Antoine Joulie
comments.body:
Thanks for the detailed answer, I'll have a look to the spec. I got you on the stale report.

My case reproduces when the primary node become isolated from the client. 

Here the step to reproduce with some more details/log

Step to reproduce: 
 1 - start a replicat set with 3 nodes. In my case I have antoines-macbook-pro.local:27019, antoines-macbook-pro.local:27021, antoines-macbook-pro.local:27022. antoines-macbook-pro.local:27021 is primary
 2 - launch my little application. Log show the driver getting the replica set topology.
 3 - from primary node console, remove the two secondary nodes. In my case I ran {{rs.remove("antoines-macbook-pro.local:27022")}} and {{rs.remove("antoines-macbook-pro.local:27019")}}. Logs show those two servers being removed from the cluster
 4 - from primary node console, re-add the two nodes. I my case I ran {{rs.add("antoines-macbook-pro.local:27022")}} and {{rs.add("antoines-macbook-pro.local:27019")}}. Logs *does not* shows those two server
[...truncated 3060 chars]

comments.author: Emily Stolfo
comments.body:
Hi [~ajoulie]

Thanks so much for the detailed description, I was able to reproduce what you are seeing. I've made a small change to the replica set topology code that should fix the issue. Would you be able to try it out in [this pull request|https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-ruby-driver/pull/749]? Let me know if it works for you.

Thanks again!

Emily

comments.author: Emily Stolfo
comments.body:
Hi [~bigbourin@gmail.com]

Thanks for reporting this. I've found that there is an oversight with passing client options through when doing a #count or #distinct command on a collection.
In my testing, I've confirmed that the read preference is applied for a #find though. Can you think of any reason why the read preference isn't applied in your #find example?

As a workaround, you can pass the read preference to the #count method and I'm seeing that work:
https://gist.github.com/estolfo/f3983378ac272cb6faaca7960f386631

I'm testing against driver version 2.2.5 in that gist. Let me know if you can see why you may be experiencing different behavior. In the meantime, I'll open a pull request to fix #distinct and #count to use the read preference from the client.

Thanks
Emily

comments.author: Adrien Jarthon
comments.body:
You're welcome.

It is working fine with {{.find}} for me, that's why I showed it in the example, it's hitting 10.10.11.8 which is the secondary.

Ok I see the hackish syntax to pass the read preference to count wasn't the correct one, I just guessed it from the ticket as it's undocumented ;)
But anyway I'm using Mongoid so I can't use that because the Mongoid's {{.count}} doesn't accept any arguments.

comments.author: Adrien Jarthon
comments.body: I would like to add that we just got the error in production again and we had to kill the worker after 5 minutes because it didn't recover this time and failed about 1500 time in a row. (last time it recovered after a few seconds). So we'll have to change our guard clause to terminate the process now as we don't know if it can recover.

comments.author: Emily Stolfo
comments.body:
Hi [~bigbourin@gmail.com]

Can you send your mongoid.yml file, with sensitive information taken out, and tell me which client you're using from the yml when you get these errors?
Also, if you could send any relevant sidekiq config information, and some sample code from the workers, that'd be helpful as well.

Thanks
Emily

comments.author: Adrien Jarthon
comments.body:
Sure, here is the mongoid.yml:
{code}
production:
  clients:
    default:
      hosts:
        - mongo1.dimelo2:27017
        - mongo2.dimelo2:27017
      database: engagement_console_production
      options:
        max_pool_size: 30
        truncate_logs: false
  options:
    preload_models: true
{code}

Here is the sidekiq.yml:
{code}
:verbose: true
:concurrency: 1
:queues:
  - high
  - medium
  - low
  - no_concurrency
{code}

Nothing weird here, it's a single threaded Sidekiq worker, because some gem we use are not thread-safe. We have another instance for multi-threaded workers but so far only the single-threaded one failed.

About the workers it's a bit complicated, we have a base class adding our domain scoping logic to the perform method (you can see here the guard clause I added to protect our workers against this bug):
{code}

    if @attributes.has_key? :domain_id
      domain = Domain.find(@attributes[:domain_id])
      ra

comments.author: Vincent Boisard
comments.body:
Hi!

I'm a colleague of Adrien who reported the bug, and I have good news: We have a way to reproduce the problem independently from our app!

This snippet does the following:
# Connect to mongo and insert documents in two distinct collections
# Spawn a thread in which we query the document in the 1st collection, with a Mongo {{sleep}} to simulate a long running query
# Kill the thread before the ruby driver has time to consume the response from the server
# Trigger a query on the 2nd collection (also with {{eval}} to have a consistent type of result)
# Check if the document we got from #4 is indeed for collection 2

I reproduce the issue 100% of the time with this code, but depending on your environment you might need to run the script a few time.

{code:ruby}
require 'mongo'

# Setup
client = Mongo::Client.new([ 'localhost:27017' ], :database => 'thread_safe_demo')
client['collection_a'].insert_one({ message: "I'm from collection A"} )
client['collection_b'].insert
[...truncated 5474 chars]

comments.author: Emily Stolfo
comments.body:
Hi [~bigbourin@gmail.com] and [~boisard.v@gmail.com]

Firstly, thank you for taking the time to debug and provide a reproducible code block showing the issue. I appreciate your help!

The fundamental problem here is that, somewhere in your application, threads are being killed without having been allowed to complete their execution. Unfortunately, Ruby does have a #kill method on its Thread objects, which allows a Thread to be terminated, regardless of the state of internal structures, what locks it holds, its completion status, etc.

Python, for example, doesn't allow a thread to be killed. It's incredibly dangerous, as you don't know the state of internal structures and you'll almost certainly crash or deadlock sometime after killing a thread. Java has a stop() function that has been deprecated. See the warning [here|https://docs.oracle.com/javase/7/docs/api/java/lang/Thread.html#stop()] for a longer explanation as to why this is unsafe. It is better instead to use the [interru
[...truncated 1486 chars]

comments.author: Adrien Jarthon
comments.body:
Thanks for your understanding, indeed this is a edge case, but the consequences are critical and could easily be avoided.

I don't think this is only a matter of ruby allowing thread killing though, *any ruby exception* raised before the response is read in this yield would also release the connection and allow for this bug. So this is not about {{Thread.kill}}, It is simply about handling errors properly. Thread kill is *one way* to reproduce this error that we managed to find but there can be many.
And indeed as I explained, we might have a lot of killed threads in our test because we start and stop EM a lot, which is totally normal and expected. But in our production Sidekiq worker, we don't use EM and we don't kill Thread, so the reason might be somewhere else.

Anyway the question is not why we kill threads, the driver should simply be more robust and don't assume that everything goes as planned IMO.

comments.author: Emily Stolfo
comments.body:
Hi [~bigbourin@gmail.com]

The driver does indeed close sockets when there is an Exception:
In the Connection class, the [#deliver method|https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-ruby-driver/blob/master/lib/mongo/server/connection.rb#L156-L159] calls #write and then #read. Both the [#write|https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-ruby-driver/blob/master/lib/mongo/server/connection.rb#L179] and [#read|https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-ruby-driver/blob/master/lib/mongo/server/connectable.rb#L106-L108] methods send/receive on the socket in a method called #ensure_connected. [This method|https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-ruby-driver/blob/master/lib/mongo/server/connectable.rb#L87-L96] will [close the socket, set it to nil and raise the Exception|https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-ruby-driver/blob/master/lib/mongo/server/connection.rb#L78-L84] if there is one so it won't be used again.

Do you see a way that an Exception when sending/receiving on a socket would allow it to be returned to the connection po
[...truncated 205 chars]

comments.author: Adrien Jarthon
comments.body: Ok Interesting, this seems well protected, I haven't investigated everything but I guess that if {{connect!}} method raises (after the socket is connected, so in {{authenticate!}} for example), this could check in an open but unauthenticated socket to the pool If I'm correct.

comments.author: Emily Stolfo
comments.body: Can you elaborate on that? If #authenticate! fails, the Exception will be caught [here|https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-ruby-driver/blob/master/lib/mongo/server/connectable.rb#L92-L95]. Let me know if you see a way for the socket to get checked back into the pool, unauthenticated.

comments.body:
Hi Emily!

From what I can tell, {{#authenticate!}} is called within {{#connect!}}.
{{#connect!}} is outside the {{begin...rescue}} block in {{#ensure_connected}}, so it would not be rescued by the code you linked.

comments.author: Vincent Boisard
comments.body:
Hi Emily,

Indeed, the exception would bubble to the app. However, if the same connection is re-used afterwards (after an over-zealous {{rescue}} for example), then we would still end up reading the response to the previous query.
I agree that this is unlikely since a failure in {{#authenticate!}} is almost always going to be related to an authentication problem, which would keep failing. 
However it might also be some kind of {{IOError}} in case of a network glitch.

Anyway, in my opinion this all boils down to the original issue: if there's no way for the driver to return the response to the wrong query, then all of this discussion about exceptions and thread safety becomes much less critical.

I haven't really found any high level specs in the driver's code where I could simulate the problem we experienced in order to validate my fix and avoid future regression. If you point me in the right direction, I'll be happy to submit a PR with the patch!

hmm, I'm not seeing in the code how you could get into the scenario you describe but if you can create a test case showing it, that would be really helpful so I can continue investigating.

[This commit|https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-ruby-driver/commit/0e6fd78c9d6c6c0cf50876172636fa2f4920d54d], introduces a similar change to the code so you might want to use it as guidance.

I've also talked with my Python driver colleagues and they do something else in their driver that can help in case a thread is killed without being able to finish reading off its socket. They check a socket when it's checked out that it's not already closed and if it has data to read. [Here|https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-python-driver/blob/master/pymongo/pool.py#L654-L677]

Thanks for the update, [~emily.stolfo]. 

That spec looks good to me. 

I was a bit confused by the difference between "n_matched" and "n", but I think I understand now (correct me if I'm wrong): For an update, 'n_matched' is the number of _persisted_ documents selected for update, and 'n' is the number of documents (persisted or not) selected for update. Thanks again,
John

comments.author: Emily Stolfo
comments.body:
Hi [~john.shearar]

There are two separate objects here: the raw response from the server after a bulk update operation and the BulkWrite::Result object, constructed by the Ruby driver.
The response from the server has the [following fields|https://docs.mongodb.org/manual/reference/command/update/#output]. You can see in that documentation, that {{n}} is "the number of documents selected for update. If the update operation results in no change to the document, e.g. $set expression updates the value to the current value, n can be greater than nModified."

In the BulkWrite::Result object (documentation of the shell equivalent is [here|https://docs.mongodb.org/manual/reference/method/WriteResult/#properties]), the {{n_matched}} field is the number of documents selected for update. {{n}} is not exposed in the BulkWrite::Result object. The distinction between {{n_matched}} and the other fields is relevant when a document is matched by the update query expression, but the update does no
[...truncated 274 chars]

comments.author: Emily Stolfo
comments.body:
Hi [~batessr@upmc.edu]

Thank you for the additional information.

It sounds like you are hitting this server bug: https://jira.mongodb.org/browse/SERVER-20335

If you could answer the following questions, that would help me verify whether this is the case:
* When did this error start happening? Were there any changes to your cluster recently? 
* How many mongoses are you connecting to?
* Can you verify the version of each mongos? This may be a server bug if the version is greater than 3.1.8, the fix version of that ticket.

In the meantime, to avoid the error, you can simply not set a limit default of 0 in the code snippet you've provided, as [a limit value of zero is equivalent to setting no limit|https://docs.mongodb.com/manual/reference/method/cursor.limit/#zero-value].

Thanks
Emily

comments.author: Bernie Hackett
comments.body: I'm not sure why Emily closed the pull request. The driver will never implement OP_COMMAND (it's being removed from the server in 4.2). Perhaps she meant OP_MSG, which doesn't have this limitation. Regardless, for server versions that don't support OP_MSG, but do support write commands, and write commands are the only place this really matters, this change is necessary. MongoDB 2.6 to 3.4.

comments.author: Oleg Pudeyev
comments.body: So the change here should be to bump a limit somewhere in the driver without adding any OP_COMMAND-related code?

comments.author: Bernie Hackett
comments.body: Yes. When encoding a command using OP_QUERY, the encoded size cannot exceed 16MB + 16k. According to this ticket the driver raises if the encoded size is greater than 16MB.

comments.body: Thanks Bernie.

comments.author: Emily Stolfo
comments.body:
Hi [~vo.x]

I'm sorry to hear that you're seeing the bson tests fail on Fedora PPC. We're looking into it and will have an update soon.
Thanks for your patience,

Emily

comments.author: Emily Stolfo
comments.body:
Hi [~vo.x]

I've been testing the bson specs on a Big Endian system, RHEL 7.2 Z-Series [here|https://evergreen.mongodb.com/task/bson_ruby_rhelzseries_build_232650b402efd3ce01f972da55c42804a81f432a_16_12_05_17_38_57]. I've targeted the tests you've said are failing on Fedora PPC: Integer, Time, and Timestamp. They pass for me. Is there any reason you can think of that I'm seeing the tests pass and you're seeing them fail?
Thanks
Emily

comments.author: Vít Ondruch
comments.body: Interesting ... I was building on PPC. I might try Z series as well .... but since there are now failing like half of the tests where previously failed just a few, it looks like the platform might not be properly detected? I'll try to take a second look tomorrow and discuss with our alternative platform experts ...

comments.author: Bernie Hackett
comments.body: [~vo.x], you're testing on PPC big endian? Can you provide output from uname -a? We have builders here to test Power 8 (ppc64le), but no big endian PPC machines. Maybe the build system isn't detecting endianness properly so the extensions aren't actually doing byte swap when appropriate.

comments.author: Bernie Hackett
comments.body:
There is an assumption here that BYTE_ORDER will always be defined (presumably provided by ruby.h?):

https://github.com/mongodb/bson-ruby/blob/master/ext/bson/native-endian.h#L38-L44

Perhaps BYTE_ORDER is undefined when the extension builds on your system?

comments.author: Vít Ondruch
comments.body:
bq. Can you provide output from uname -a? 

{noformat}
$ uname -a
Linux buildppc-03.phx2.fedoraproject.org 4.7.2-201.fc24.ppc64 #1 SMP Tue Aug 30 08:24:40 UTC 2016 ppc64 ppc64 ppc64 GNU/Linux
{noformat}

Now I noticed that bson 4.2.0 has 10x more tests then the 4.1.1 version, that might be the main reason for more failures ... There is several pages [1] of "Decimal128" test errors which is new feature of 4.2.0.



[1] https://kojipkgs.fedoraproject.org//work/tasks/6046/16786046/build.log

comments.author: Vít Ondruch
comments.body: I got support from Fedora's alternative platform experts. It really seems to be issue with the BYTE_ORDER macro. It seems to be supported on BSD-like systems, but \_\_BYTE_ORDER\_\_ macro should be used elsewhere. Please follow [this|https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=1400147#c5] ticket for more details.

comments.author: Vít Ondruch
comments.body:
Ok, several things:

1) This [patch|https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=1400147#c7] proposed by Dan Horák seems to correctly detect the endianness (although Dan has small bug there):

{code:diff}
--- ext/bson/native-endian.h.orig	2016-12-07 13:28:27.715599434 +0100
+++ ext/bson/native-endian.h	2016-12-07 13:56:53.564406614 +0100
@@ -36,11 +36,19 @@
 #endif
 
 #ifndef BSON_BYTE_ORDER
+#if defined(BYTE_ORDER)
 # if BYTE_ORDER == LITTLE_ENDIAN
 #  define BSON_BYTE_ORDER 1234
 # elif BYTE_ORDER == BIG_ENDIAN
 #  define BSON_BYTE_ORDER 4321
 # endif
+#elif defined(__BYTE_ORDER__)
+# if __BYTE_ORDER__ == __ORDER_LITTLE_ENDIAN__
+#  define BSON_BYTE_ORDER 1234
+# elif __BYTE_ORDER__ == __ORDER_BIG_ENDIAN__
+#  define BSON_BYTE_ORDER 4321
+# endif
+#endif
 #endif
 
 #if defined(__sun)
{code}

This makes the 4.1.1 build pass on LE as well as BE platforms.

2) You introduced support for Decimal128 in bson 4.2.0, but there does not appear to be support for B
[...truncated 864 chars]

comments.author: Vít Ondruch
comments.body:
Actually this might be better version of the patch, since it seems that BYTE_ORDER is obsolete, where \__BYTE_ORDER__ is the new and shiny thing for GCC:

{code:diff}
--- ext/bson/native-endian.h.orig	2016-12-07 13:28:27.715599434 +0100
+++ ext/bson/native-endian.h	2016-12-07 13:56:53.564406614 +0100
@@ -36,11 +36,19 @@
 #endif
 
 #ifndef BSON_BYTE_ORDER
+#if defined(__BYTE_ORDER__)
+# if __BYTE_ORDER__ == __ORDER_LITTLE_ENDIAN__
+#  define BSON_BYTE_ORDER 1234
+# elif __BYTE_ORDER__ == __ORDER_BIG_ENDIAN__
+#  define BSON_BYTE_ORDER 4321
+# endif
+#elif defined(BYTE_ORDER)
 # if BYTE_ORDER == LITTLE_ENDIAN
 #  define BSON_BYTE_ORDER 1234
 # elif BYTE_ORDER == BIG_ENDIAN
 #  define BSON_BYTE_ORDER 4321
 # endif
+#endif
 #endif
 
 #if defined(__sun)
{code}

comments.author: Emily Stolfo
comments.body:
Hi [~vo.x]

Thank you for looking into this and informing us of your test results. I've been talking with our c team as well and they have some suggestions after taking a look at how [Ruby itself determines byte order|https://github.com/ruby/ruby/blob/trunk/siphash.c#L8-L18]. I'll have a patch ready soon for testing and it'd be great to have you take a look at it when I'm done.

Thanks again
 Emily

comments.author: Bernie Hackett
comments.body:
Similar to Python, Ruby also, in some cases, defines WORDS_BIGENDIAN:

https://github.com/ruby/ruby/blob/202bbda2bf5f25343e286099140fb9282880ecba/include/ruby/defines.h#L232-L237

The sha2 module has a simple test for endianness:

https://github.com/ruby/ruby/blob/202bbda2bf5f25343e286099140fb9282880ecba/ext/digest/sha2/sha2.h#L58-L65

comments.author: Durran Jordan
comments.body:
As a note, we should probably be doing what libbson does, which is here:

https://github.com/mongodb/libbson/blob/master/src/bson/bson-endian.h#L43

Note that they also had similar issues as of late and resolved them as noted here:

https://github.com/mongodb/libbson/commit/28fae992db83bfb1858271f75593413b1a05e290

comments.author: Oleg Pudeyev
comments.body:
It looks like the driver already attempts to provide some level of compatibility with old server versions for explain commands. As I see it the options are as follows:

1. When the result of the explain looks like a pre-4.2 result (we handle two different variations in pre-4.2 world), apply existing field extraction behavior for compatibility purposes. Otherwise return the response unmodified. This should make the driver behave the same on pre-4.2 servers, and provide compatibility with 4.2 servers also. Since the output format would be different between pre-4.2 and 4.2 anyway, application code would have to account for both possibilities regardless. This route seems to be most in line with how the driver is currently written.
2. When the result of the explain looks like a 4.2 result, return the entire result unmodified. Otherwise apply existing logic to extract fields out of the result.
3. When talking to a 4.2+ server, return the top level result of the explain. When talking to o
[...truncated 346 chars]

comments.author: Oleg Pudeyev
comments.body: We decided to go with the first option in the previous comment.

comments.author: Sven Riedel
comments.body:
Just stumbled over this as well (mongo gem 2.13.1, ruby 2.7.1)

 

The issue is that in [https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-ruby-driver/blob/master/lib/mongo/cursor/builder/get_more_command.rb#L68] the await_data option must also be set in the view, however when creating the watcher instance (here for the database, the watchers for the client and collection have all the same issue) [https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-ruby-driver/blob/master/lib/mongo/database.rb#L413] no options are passed to the view at all.

Essentially, the watch method should check if max_await_time_ms has been given as an option and if so, pass both max_await_time_ms and await_data: true options to the initializer of the view.

 

I've attached a script which demonstrates the problem and the fix with a dirty PoC fix (please don't use this in production code!)

comments.author: Oleg Pudeyev
comments.body: Per the above comment it appears the issue affects database.watch also, not just collection.watch.

comments.author: Sven Riedel
comments.body: client.watch as well. Essentially any watch method where the view is passed to the Mongo::Collection::View::ChangeStream constructor without prior evaluation of the options given to the watch method, and where a max_await_time_ms option is documented: Mongo::Client#watch, Mongo::Database#watch and Mongo::Collection#watch

[^RUBY-2095-recreate-sidekiq-issue.zip]

^Here you go.  I realized in the process that it appears that the issue is actually in the mongo_session_store gem, not necessarily the mongoid or BSON gems.  I'm assuming I'll need to report the bug to them, but wanted to post the code here just in case it is actually a mongoid issue that's surfacing weirdly via the mongo_session_store gem.^

comments.author: Brad Wedell
Brad Wedell
comments.body:
And did a little more digging and pinpointed where in the mongo_session_store gem the problem was:

 [https://github.com/mongoid/mongo_session_store/blob/master/lib/mongo_session_store/mongo_store_base.rb#L49]

 

I added a monkey patch to config/initializers/rails_session_store_monkey_patch.rb:
{code:java}
module ActionDispatch
  module Session
    class MongoStoreBase < AbstractStore
      def find_or_initialize_session(id)
        # Fixes bson type issue
        existing_session = (id && session_class.where(:_id => id.to_s).first)
        session = existing_session if existing_session
        session ||= session_class.new(:_id => generate_sid)
        [session._id, session]
      end
    end
  end
end
{code}
The key here was changing the ".where(:_id => id)" to ".where(:_id => id.to_s)".  I have no idea why changing the rack version seemed to have fixed things originally.  Just seems really weird that upgrading our gems would yield a regression like this.  I ha
[...truncated 113 chars]

[^RUBY-2095-sidekiq-issue-monkey-patched.zip]

Here's the version that's working with the monkey patch for reference.

comments.author: Alexey Zapparov
comments.body: The change in Time serialization was introduced in: https://github.com/mongodb/bson-ruby/pull/160

comments.author: Oleg Pudeyev
comments.body: It seems that truncating the time to the nearest millisecond rather than rounding it would produce the desired behavior, do you agree [~alexey@zapparov.com]?

comments.author: Alexey Zapparov
comments.body:
Yes, I believe that's the right way of describing the expectations. Here's patch that outlines the desires:
{code:diff}diff --git a/lib/bson/time.rb b/lib/bson/time.rb
index d3c2f76..9c325ea 100644
--- a/lib/bson/time.rb
+++ b/lib/bson/time.rb
@@ -39,9 +39,13 @@ module BSON
     # @since 2.0.0
     def to_bson(buffer = ByteBuffer.new, validating_keys = Config.validating_keys?)
       # A previous version of this method used the following implementation:
-      # buffer.put_int64((to_i * 1000) + (usec / 1000))
+      #   buffer.put_int64((to_i * 1000) + (usec / 1000))
       # Turns out, usec returned incorrect value - 999 for 1 millisecond.
-      buffer.put_int64((to_f * 1000).round)
+      # But next version was not good either:
+      #   buffer.put_int64((to_f * 1000).round)
+      # Float is not capable of correctly holding nanoseconds either and
+      # rounding it up leads to unexpected results sometimes.
+      buffer.put_int64((to_r * 1000).to_i)
     end
 
[...truncated 730 chars]

comments.author: Tom Scott
comments.body:
we're also experiencing this issue:

 
{code:java}
end_of_today = Time.current.end_of_day.utc
# => 2020-03-02 23:59:59 UTC
start_of_tomo = (Time.current + 1.day).beginning_of_day.utc
# => 2020-03-03 00:00:00 UTC
end_of_today.to_f
# => 1583193600.0
start_of_tomo.to_f
# => 1583193600.0
(end_of_today.to_i * 1000) + (end_of_today.usec / 1000)
# => 1583193599999
(end_of_today.to_f * 1000).round
# => 1583193600000
(start_of_tomo.to_f * 1000).round
# => 1583193600000 {code}
 

...and have been able to fix it by reverting to the original implementation of {{#to_bson}}: https://github.com/workarea-commerce/workarea/pull/382

comments.author: Oleg Pudeyev
comments.body: JRuby does not correctly handle pre-epoch timestamps (it rounds up rather than down): https://github.com/jruby/jruby/issues/6104

comments.author: Oleg Pudeyev
comments.body:
Hi [~jon@braze.com], thank you for the report.

The contents of a request (i.e. whether it includes $readPreference) is a separate matter from the server selected for the request. A secondary read is one that is sent to a secondary, the contents of the request is immaterial at this point. Can you clarify whether you are reporting a problem in server selection (queries directed to primaries) or in request contents ($readPreference field missing when you think it should be included)?

comments.author: Jon Hyman
comments.body:
Hi Oleg,

I am in a sharded topology so all the Server objects are mongoS instances. From my understanding, server selection returns all servers as candidates in a sharded topology.

What I do know is that on 2.14.0, 100% of our \{read: {mode: :secondary_preferred}} queries go to primaries. If I patch Mongo::Operation::OpMsgBase or SessionsSupported method #add_read_preference to use #to_doc instead of #to_mongos in the connection.description.mongos? statement, it properly corrects the issue and we see secondary_preferred queries going to secondaries. Without this change, we cannot upgrade to the driver as it is overloading our primaries. That is, this change fixes the issue for me.

 
{code:ruby}elsif connection.description.mongos?
      # When server is a mongos:
      # - $readPreference is never sent when mode is 'primary'
      # - When mode is 'secondaryPreferred' $readPreference is only sent
      #   when a non-mode field (i.e. tag_sets) is present
      # - Otherwi
[...truncated 75 chars]

comments.author: Oleg Pudeyev
comments.body: I reproduced the issue on 4.0 using secondary preferred read preference.

comments.author: Oleg Pudeyev
comments.body: :secondary read preference appears to be working correctly, and replica sets appear to also be working correctly. The problem appears to be only with :secondary_preferred read preference in sharded clusters and only for OP_MSG (3.6+) servers.

comments.author: Oleg Pudeyev
comments.body:
According to the information I received, service id does not necessarily identify a service. For example, multiple services may report the same service id so that when any of them fails, the driver would close connections to all services.

We should be able to pin to connection identifiers instead of service_ids. Doing so will permit retaining connection ownership by connection pool and we will retain the existing block scope for connection checkouts.

We may need to come up with a unique identifier for connections. The server + connection id combination might work since no server discovery is happening with load balancers. Failing that, perhaps a global registry or counter would work, similar to how the current connection ids are generated. Ruby's object_id is tempting but it could be reused by future objects.

comments.author: Oleg Pudeyev
comments.body:
This change broke Mongoid because in Mongoid, #upsert is actually using replace_one instead of update_one (per https://jira.mongodb.org/browse/MONGOID-5363) but Mongoid invokes update_one method with the replacement document, and such invocation is prohibited by this ticket (RUBY-2996).

I think the validation added in this ticket needs to be conditional on Mongoid not being loaded (e.g. `if !defined(::Mongoid)`) to not break all Mongoid operations.

https://jira.mongodb.org/browse/MONGOID-5363 covers potentially changing Mongoid to perform an update when it does an upsert (or do so by default).

comments.author: Oleg Pudeyev
comments.body: This is also a breaking change for the driver itself, per https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-ruby-driver/pull/2507.

comments.author: Thomas LI
comments.body: Thanks again for fixing this issue we reported. Since this bug affects the stability of our upgrade and maintenance workflows, could you share an estimated timeline for the next release that will include this fix? If possible, we’d also appreciate knowing whether a patch release is planned, as it would help us determine whether to wait or temporarily pin to a specific commit.

comments.author: Dmitry Rybakov
comments.body: Hi [~thomas.li@shoplineapp.com], thank you for reporting the issue! I've just created a release candidate PR, and I believe we release a version with the fix this week.

comments.author: Thomas LI
comments.body: Thanks for the update and for moving quickly on this. That timeline works great for us. We’ll keep an eye out for the release candidate and the upcoming version.

comments.author: Dmitry Rybakov
comments.body: Hi [~thomas.li@shoplineapp.com], unfortunately, the new version will be released only on Monday. I forgot to pull the trigger yesterday, and we do not make releases on Fridays. Sorry for the delay!

comments.author: Emily Stolfo
comments.body:
Hi [~matt.parlane@gmail.com]

I've looked into this and wanted to give you an update.
The problem is in the bson_ext where we don't properly convert the module constants to the correct types in C. The checks here:
https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-ruby-driver/blob/1.x-stable/ext/cbson/cbson.c#L262-L291

aren't producing the correct results because the flags defined here are not the correct C type.
https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-ruby-driver/blob/1.x-stable/ext/cbson/cbson.c#L1159-L1164

I'll have a fix soon.
Thanks again

Emily

comments.author: Emily Stolfo
comments.body: fyi, driver 2.0 does not have this problem.

comments.author: Durran Jordan
comments.body: Could you open a pull request to https://github.com/mongodb/bson-ruby ? Then I'll merge the changes in and do a release.

comments.author: Durran Jordan
comments.body:
I've merged in the pull request, and needed to remove the endian.h include since it won't compile on OSX, but sys/types.h already includes it. The only thing I need to make sure is that this works on windows. Do you have a Windows box to test on?

Or,
[~behackett] do we have a windows machine we can verify this change on before releasing?

comments.author: Bernie Hackett
comments.body: The Windows slaves on Jenkins. [~craiggwilson] or [~luke.lovett] can help.

comments.author: Jeff Blight
comments.body: Hi, I have re-tested with the changes made for OSX support and everything is working as expected. Do you know when these changes will be incorporated into an updated gem?

comments.author: Durran Jordan
comments.body: It's been released in bson 3.2.3.

comments.author: Jeff Blight
comments.body: Thank you
